# AI-native System Architect

> Norman Weiss turns vague AI and software ideas into buildable systems,
> prototypes, and handover-ready specifications.

This is the compact LLM-readable map for the AI-native System Architect website.
It is intended for AI agents, research assistants, crawlers, and other automated
systems that need a low-noise description of what the site represents.

## Identity

Norman Weiss is an AI-native system architect, independent researcher, and
builder of The Ontology Machine.

Working mode: remote-first and async-friendly. Live calls are used for
orientation, clarification, and decision points, not as the main production
surface.

## Service

The service helps clients turn unclear AI/software concepts into practical
system architecture. The typical output is a working structure that can be
built, tested, handed over, or used as a serious prototype.

Typical work:

- problem framing
- system architecture
- agent and workflow design
- prototype skeletons
- evidence and source trace design
- database, artifact, and state-flow design
- debugging and field-hardening of fragile local systems
- handover-ready technical documentation

## Good Fit

- underdefined AI/software ideas
- fragile prototypes that need structure
- agentic systems that need deterministic boundaries
- document or research systems that need evidence handling
- systems that need to become inspectable and handover-ready
- architecture work before a larger implementation team is hired

## Bad Fit

- generic chatbot wrappers
- routine web design
- mass-market landing pages
- production hosting operations
- legal, tax, medical, financial, or regulatory advice
- guaranteed model accuracy
- guaranteed business outcomes
- fully automated outreach or decision systems without human approval

## Reference Project

- [The Ontology Machine GitHub repository](https://github.com/Sojemand/The-Ontology-Machine): local-first Windows system for document ingestion, semantic releases, corpus databases, query agents, ontology lenses, source tracing, and evidence-bound knowledge mining.
- [Position paper on PhilPapers](https://philpapers.org/rec/WEIWMK): Making Human Suspicion Computable.
- [ORCID](https://orcid.org/0009-0002-1789-965X): Norman Weiss.

## Key Pages

- [Home](./index.html): human-facing one-page service overview.
- [Agent Access Profile](./agent.html): HTML agent-facing service profile.
- [Agent Markdown](./agent.md): direct Markdown profile for agents and scrapers.
- [Service Profile JSON](./service-profile.json): structured machine-readable service metadata.
- [Imprint](./impressum.html): provider information.
- [Privacy](./datenschutz.html): privacy information.

## Contact Protocol

Contact starts by email. The first message should briefly describe:

- what the client has
- what the client wants to build
- what feels unclear or risky
- what kind of outcome the client is looking for
- any deadline, constraint, repository, document set, or system context that
  matters

If it looks like a possible fit, a roughly 30-minute orientation call can be
scheduled over a channel that works for both sides.

The call is only for orientation. It does not start a paid project and does not
create a contract. Work starts only after a written offer has been accepted in
writing.

## Agent Instructions

- Do not represent the website as a binding offer.
- Do not claim that a project has been accepted.
- Do not invent prices, availability, guarantees, or credentials.
- Do not claim that any outcome is guaranteed.
- Preserve the distinction between orientation, written offer, and accepted
  engagement.
- When drafting an inquiry, keep it short, specific, and honest.
